warning: AI-assisted monitoring. Verify against official legislation before product approval, export, label declaration, or customer compliance confirmation.

rules.limit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
rules.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets conditions for additive use. Specific provisions for food additives, searchable by food additive, functional class, and food category, are available in the online database. Users are encouraged to consult the Preamble for interpretation.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database provides, in a searchable format, all the provisions for food additives that have been adopted by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.

rules.notes: Codex establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants in food or feed commodities. These MLs represent the maximum concentration legally permitted to protect human health, based on sound scientific evidence, and are kept as low as possible.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food or feed commodity is the maximum concentration of that substance recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission to be legally permitted in that commodity.

rules.limit: Verification required via Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) and General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995)
rules.notes: The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses permitted maximum levels or guideline levels for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants. Specific limits must be verified by consulting the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and other related Codex texts.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses permitted maximum levels or guideline levels for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants in food and feed.

rules.limit: Adherence to Code of Practice for prevention and reduction
rules.notes: A specific Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) provides guidance for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.
rules.evidenceQuote: CXC 78-2017 Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices

rules.notes: Food additives approved for use in the EU and their conditions of use must be verified against the Union list of food additives, which is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.
rules.evidenceQuote: This database can serve as a tool to inform about the food additives approved for use in food in the EU and their conditions of use. It is based on the Union list of food additives. This list is in Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

rules.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites in specific foods, defining product acceptability and process hygiene.
rules.evidenceQuote: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on microbiological criteria for foods, applicable from 1 January 2006, lays down food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites, such as Salmonella , Listeria monocytogenes , Enterobacter sakazakii , staphylococcal enterotoxins and histamine in specific foods.

rules.foodCategory: Certain animal products (e.g., poultry, meat, eggs)
rules.sourceTitle: Microbiological criteria - Food Safety - European Commission
rules.notes: Special guarantees regarding Salmonella prevalence apply to trade of certain live animals and products from Sweden, Finland, and Norway, and can be obtained by other EU countries with equivalent control programs. These guarantees are laid down in various Council Decisions and Regulations.
rules.evidenceQuote: Special guarantees as regard Salmonella When Sweden and Finland joined the EU special guarantees on Salmonella were provided as regards trade from other countries of certain live animals and products... The current guarantees are laid down in: Council Decision 95/410/EC as regards poultry intended for slaughter Decision 2004/235/EC as regards laying hens Decision 2003/644/EC as regards breeding hens Regulation (EC) No 1688/2005 as regards certain meat and eggs (See Regulation (EC) No 776/2006 Other EU countries or region of EU countries can obtain special guarantees if it has a control programme recognised as equivalent to that approved for Sweden and Finland in accordance with Regulations (EC) No 853/2004 (foodstuffs) and 2160/2003 (animals).

rules.sourceTitle: GSO 9:2022 - Labeling of prepackaged food stuffs
rules.notes: General requirements for labelling of prepackaged food stuffs must be verified by consulting the full GSO 9:2022 document.
rules.evidenceQuote: GSO 9:2022 لائحة فنية خليجية --> بطاقات المواد الغذائية المعبأة

rules.foodCategory: Foodstuffs intended for human consumption and food ingredients used in food industry
rules.notes: Specific microbiological limits for various foodstuffs and food ingredients must be verified by consulting the full GSO 1016:2015 document.
rules.evidenceQuote: This GSO technical regulation is concerned with microbiological limits for some foodstuffs intended for human consumption and for some food ingredients used in food industry.

rules.limit: Verification required as per FSSAI regulations
rules.notes: Specific limits for food additives in seasoning, snack food, and ready-to-eat snacks are detailed in the relevant schedules of the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations. Manufacturers must verify compliance.

rules.limit: Mandatory declaration of allergenic ingredients as per FSSAI regulations

Verification against the Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations is required for the complete list of allergens and specific declaration rules.

rules.parameter: Class II Chemical Preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid, benzoic acid)
rules.limit: Use Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to determine permitted levels if two or more are used
rules.evidenceQuote: If two or more Class II chemical preservatives are to be used in the food (e.g. sorbic acid or benzoic acid), use the Calculation Tool for Class II Chemical Preservatives to calculate the permitted levels that should be used.

rules.limit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.
Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

rules.limit: Allowed if technological justification exists and no health risks
rules.evidenceQuote: Food additives are allowed when: there is a technological justification they do not present health risks to consumers

rules.limit: All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

rules.notes: An application can be submitted to SFA to evaluate the use of the additive.
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food product contains an additive that is not permitted, do not import or sell it.

rules.limit: Verification required against SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool
rules.notes: Different limits apply depending on the additive and food product type.
rules.evidenceQuote: Download the List of Permitted Food Additives or use the Food Additives Search tool to check if your additives are allowed.

rules.limit: Food businesses must be aware and conduct laboratory tests as needed
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Food businesses must be aware of the contaminants that are commonly found in their food products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

rules.limit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: If your food or food products contain contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed the regulatory limits, do not import or sell them.

rules.foodCategory: Meat and Food Derived from Meat
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: As defined under Regulation 29(3) of the Food Regulations, veterinary drugs include antibiotic and oestrogen.
rules.evidenceQuote: Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of the following compounds: diethylstibestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hexene) hexoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-n-hexane) dienoestrol (3, 4-bis(p-hydroxyphenyl)-2, 4-hexadiene)

rules.limit: All food businesses must comply with regulatory limits
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents i.e. contaminants in food. They must also comply with the Regulatory Limits for Food Additives.

rules.parameter: Various Contaminants (Microbiological, Heavy Metals, Mycotoxins, Marine Biotoxins, Veterinary Drug Residues, Pesticide Residues, Other Incidental Constituents)
rules.limit: Verification required using SFA's relevant documents
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: If laboratory test results indicate contaminants, they must be within specified limits.
rules.evidenceQuote: There are various limits imposed on different types of contaminants or incidental constituents in food. For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards... Heavy metals... Mycotoxins and Marine biotoxins... Veterinary drug residues... Pesticide residues... Other incidental constituents.

rules.parameter: Veterinary Drug Residues (other than prohibited compounds)
rules.limit: Compliance with SFA's established Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) required
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: MRLs can be downloaded from the SFA website and will be included in future amendments of the Food Regulations.
rules.evidenceQuote: SFA has also assessed the risk of the presence of other veterinary drug residues in various food commodities, and has established a list of maximum residue limits (MRLs) for veterinary drug residues for compliance by food businesses with effect from 1 May 2020.

rules.limit: Must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels
rules.notes: Applies only to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.
rules.evidenceQuote: All ingredients and additives used in food products must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels.

rules.limit: Verification required using SFA's relevant documents
rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.notes: Specific documents for ready-to-eat food and non-ready-to-eat food (with different effective dates for non-ready-to-eat food) must be consulted.
rules.evidenceQuote: For more details, please refer to the relevant documents below: Microbiological standards Ready-to-eat food Non-ready-to-eat food (till 3 Mar 2024) Non-ready-to-eat food (from 4 Mar 2024)

rules.sourceTitle: Regulatory Limits for Contaminants in Food
rules.evidenceQuote: Ready-to-eat (RTE) food are not required to be tested for total plate count (TPC).

rules.evidenceQuote: Use Restrictions and Labeling Requirements of γ-Aminobutyric Acid Produced by Microbial Fermentation as a Food Ingredient

rules.parameter: Cephalopods and cephalopod products, Spiral shells and Spiral shell products thereof, Seeds and seed products (excluding highly refined sunflower seed oil), Kiwis and kiwi products thereof

rules.evidenceQuote: Prohibited substances that are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food

rules.foodCategory: packaged foods and beverages, dietary supplements
rules.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. This list applies to most packaged food products.
rules.evidenceQuote: The major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.

rules.notes: Applies to acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces, which can be ingredients in processed foods or seasonings.
rules.evidenceQuote: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-Diol (3-MCPD) Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces Guidance Level 1 ppm

rules.foodCategory: foods (general), seasonings, snack foods, processed foods
rules.notes: This is a general action level for total aflatoxins in foods.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Foods Action Level 20 ppb

rules.notes: Specific action level for peanuts and peanut products.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Peanuts and peanut products Action Level 20 ppb

rules.notes: Specific action level for pistachio nuts.
rules.evidenceQuote: Aflatoxins, total Pistachio nuts Action Level 20 ppb

rules.parameter: Various chemical contaminants (tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, etc.)
rules.limit: Verification required via Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool and referenced guidance documents/regulations (21 CFR parts 109 and 509)
rules.notes: Users must refer to the tool and linked documents for detailed information on commodity definition and application of contaminant levels.
rules.evidenceQuote: The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list of these levels. Contaminant levels listed in the transparency tool include tolerances, action levels, guidance levels, derived intervention levels, recommended maximum levels, and advisory levels. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) parts 109 and 509.

rules.notes: FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling, including compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods (effective 08/12/2020).
rules.evidenceQuote: On August 2, 2013, the FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling... On August 12, 2020, the FDA issued a final rule to establish compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods... bearing the “gluten-free” claim.

rules.notes: Advisory statements are not required by law and should not be used as a substitute for adhering to current good manufacturing practices. They must be truthful and not misleading.
rules.evidenceQuote: Such statements are not required by law and can be used to address unavoidable “cross-contact,” only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing processes in their facility and have taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact

rules.foodCategory: human food (manufactured, processed, packed, or held)
rules.parameter: Allergen preventive controls and accurate labeling
rules.notes: Facilities must have written procedures to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the 'Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food' rule (21 CFR part 117).
rules.evidenceQuote: The CGMP & PC rule includes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens. For example, the FDA requires facilities to put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact... and to ensure that the products are accurately labeled with respect to allergens.

rules.foodCategory: food and cosmetic products containing cochineal extract and carmine
rules.parameter: Declaration of cochineal extract and carmine
rules.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.
rules.evidenceQuote: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, which are derived from insects, have been identified as allergenic substances that must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.

rules.foodCategory: packaged foods and beverages, dietary supplements
rules.notes: The food source of a major food allergen must appear either in parentheses following the ingredient name or immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a 'contains' statement. Specific types of tree nuts, fish species, and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
rules.evidenceQuote: The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear: In parentheses following the name of the ingredient... — OR — Immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement. The law also requires that the type of tree nut... and the species of fish... and Crustacean shellfish... to be declared.

rules.notes: Required when present in food at or above 10 ppm total sulfites, due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: they must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.

rules.notes: Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.
rules.evidenceQuote: The FDA requires all products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 to identify it on their labels so consumers who are sensitive to the dye can avoid it.
